stem: Find the minimum distance from the point \( ([[p]],[[q]]) \) to the line \( y=x \).
Using calculus, find the positive \( x \)-coordinate of the point on the curve \( y=x^{2} \) that is closest to the point \( \left(0,\dfrac{[[h2]]}{2}\right) \) on the \( y \)-axis.
solution: Minimize \( D(x)^2=x^{2}+\left(x^{2}-\dfrac{[[h2]]}{2}\right)^{2} \). Setting the derivative to 0 and discarding \( x=0 \) gives \( x^{2}=\dfrac{[[h2]]}{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} \), so the positive root is \( x=\sqrt{\dfrac{[[h2]]-1}{2}}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This has the wrong sign; the problem asks for the positive \( x \)-coordinate.
This omits the \( -\tfrac12 \) shift from differentiating the squared term.
This drops the square root, leaving \( x^{2} \) instead of \( x \).
This drops the square root and also omits the \( -\tfrac12 \) shift.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Using calculus, find the \( x \)-coordinate of the point in the first quadrant on the curve \( xy=[[k]] \) that is closest to the origin.
solution: With \( y=[[k]]/x \), minimize \( D(x)^2=x^{2}+[[k]]^{2}/x^{2} \). Setting the derivative to 0 gives \( x^{4}=[[k]]^{2} \), so the first-quadrant solution is \( x=\sqrt{[[k]]}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports \( k \) itself rather than \( \sqrt{k} \).
This has the wrong sign for a first-quadrant point.
This confuses the distance formula's extra factor with the x-coordinate.
This halves \( k \) instead of taking a square root.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The line \( y=[[m]]x+([[b]]) \) lies entirely below the parabola \( y=x^{2}+[[c]] \) for all \( x \). Using calculus, find the minimum vertical distance between the two curves.
distractor_rationales: This has the wrong sign on the vertex-shift term.
This omits the vertex-shift term entirely.
This reports only the vertex-shift term, dropping \( c-b \).
This uses the wrong denominator (2 instead of 4).

stem: Using calculus, find the \( x \)-coordinate of the point on the line \( y=[[m]]x+([[b]]) \) that is closest to the origin.
solution: Minimize \( D(x)^2=x^{2}+([[m]]x+([[b]]))^{2} \). Setting the derivative to 0 gives \( x=\dfrac{-[[m]]\,([[b]])}{1+[[m]]^{2}}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This omits the \( 1+m^{2} \) denominator.
This has the wrong sign.
This omits the factor of \( m \) in the numerator.
This uses \( m \) as a denominator instead of \( 1+m^{2} \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
